sentences.text: No, not really.
One of the women guards used to come in and you know, she looked at the beds.
The beds had to be flat.
Doesn't matter how much luggage or whatever you put under-- under your blanket, but it had to be one straight line.
And if it wasn't, she pulled the whole thing off.
And the washing, you did your washing.
It could hang during the nights above your beds, but during the day nothing was to be seen.
What you did with it, under your blanket, as long as it was flat, one straight line.
One was hanged.
Stealing from the kitchen, what my friend did, too.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
No, I don't know.
Just happened, and we didn't see it.
But it happened.
Yeah.
I don't know.
Standing on roll call for hours and hours and hours.
Sick people.
In the beginning.
Later we had to share.
Never counted them.
I don't know.
Not in the beginning.
But later when the war started turning a bit, and they emptied camps, and they went walk about, and more and more people came in the camp.
And they had to double up.
And at the very end, you know, husbands and wives, they were together in Bergen-Belsen, but not in the same barracks, of course.
They had male and female barracks.
And then at the end, you know, the couples said, well, if we have to share with strangers, we might as well share with our husbands.
You know, they didn't care anymore at the end.
Yes.
We tend to believe it, because we knew we were going to a special camp.
Well, because of the British nationality.
We knew it was something different.
But sometimes a German would come from the higher up, and we had to come and roll call, and some people were called out, and they had to go to the Auschwitz camps.
You know, the revised papers.
And like I was a born, myself, in Scotland.
So that was all right for them.
But then you had, one of the parents was born there, and they revised that and they had to go.
If one of the parents were born in England, the parents were British, and for a while they regarded the children also.
But later on when they revised it, the children had to go.
So that happened a few times.
Outside the camp, no.
What do you mean?
We don't know what was there.
Later on we got some friends moving in in our camp.
And they still did the makeup and the lipstick.
But when they got dressed in the morning, there were long johns.
It was not combination.
I didn't care.
Did not care.
I don't think I've ever seen a mirror, to tell you the truth.
Yeah, but see, he was extra hungry because he wasn't quite normal.
And he went into the garbage.
And somebody put him in, and he got 24 hours in the brig, they called it, in prison.
Well, just isolated by himself.
And I couldn't bring him any food because the man in the tower would have seen that.
And I think then when he got pneumonia, and that gave him, you know, that did it, being on his own.
Well, that's when he died.
You know?
That helped him along.
It was no good for him.
Just before, let me think, we came there in January.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '43, uh, '44. 1944.
It was terrible.
I was called.
One of the women said, you have to come and help.
Go to [NON-ENGLISH] bed.
Let's help him tidy his bed.
His bed is in a mess.
And I went there.
He was dying.
And so, when he died, I walked behind him.
You know, they had a horse and cart, and you can walk behind it until the gates.
No, they don't put a coffin.
But some of the men said prayers for him.
And then he goes out of the gate and that's it.
Yeah, well, it happened all the time.
Happened all the time.
Yeah.
In the beginning, I wasn't used to it.
Not until later when I started working, as a-- help out as a nurse.
Then I remember when I saw my first dead person.
I saw a pair of feet sticking out of the bed.
And I know.
But later on it didn't do anything to me.
I helped them lay off, put them in the bathroom until they got them away.
I had a cousin of my mother there, and she was dying.
She wouldn't eat.
I made her a slice of bread.
I made it like a bit of cake.
I put jam on it and a little figure, [INAUDIBLE] look like a little cake, trying to feed her.
But she wouldn't, she had no will.
And one morning I came and the other nurses said she died, and we left her for you to lay off.
And it didn't do anything to me, dead people.
No.
Well, I became ill, typhoid.
All I remember is that I wasn't in my own barrack.
And one night, I had to go to the toilet so badly.
And it was a terrible weather.
And you had to go outside.
And I remember I went outside, and then I remember that somebody picked me up and said, oh, it's Lotte, it's Lotte.
And they took me to the hospital.
And I was unconscious.
And that's friend, you know, the mother of the boy, she brought me soup everyday.
And vaguely, I remember it, and the typhoid, I have to eat.
I have to eat.
And I came out of it.
And as soon as I was better, as they say, I was, I had to go to another barrack where I was used to, and I couldn't even get up the steps, I was so weak.
Anyhow, I went a few times back to the hospital and I helped out.
And I stayed there to help out.
Well, take temperatures, get them the pan, the bed pan, and just be there for them.
At the end, you know, you get big water in the legs-- fluid, fluid.
Typhoid, of course, and malnutrition-- anything.
We all got a needle against typhoid, after which we all got typhoid.
So I don't know what they gave us.
We all got typhoid after we got the needles.
Probably.
Probably.
And then, you know, when I helped out in the hospital, I saw a good friend of [NON-ENGLISH], while I nursed his mother in the other ward.
And that was terrible.
A lot of friend had typhoid.
He survived it.
That was a terrible time.
Yes.
And I had to wash them.
And the lice.
We had so much lice.
You know, everyday, we got-- we got undressed, we looked at our clothes and tried to kill, and kill with your fingers.
We thought we'll never, you know, we'll do this for our lives.
We never though, you know, it would get any better.
Because I remember, I was washing one woman, and I had a washer in my hand, and I put it on her body and there was thousands.
It was full, the washer was full.
And that was terrible.
Yeah, of course
No.
No.
Wouldn't help.
Wouldn't help.
Didn't think.
[LAUGHS] We did not think.
As long as was alive, you was alive.
I remember in the hospital, a poor mother sitting near the bed of a little child who was dying.
You know?
Little things like that.
Not really.
Yeah, there were-- our doctors.
They did what they could.
They were good doctors.
Yeah, yeah.
I don't know, conversations, but yeah.
Yeah.
Overcrowded.
That's actually when the lice set in, with the overcrowding.
Two, three in a bed.
It was terrible.
Same.
It was the same.
From other camps.
Dutch, French.
They didn't know anything because they came out of worse camps.
Nothing.
Sorry.
Terrible.
I'm not a good talker.
No.
Another day we had to leave the camp.
We went out and others came in.
And we tried to ask them how, you know so and so, you know so and so?
And bang, bang, bang.
They were hit, because we're not allowed to have contact.
Not us, but the others.
Because they came from worse camps.
No, no.
Not-- no.
Jewish people.
Well, there was Jewish people, like they had one camp [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the boss of the whole camp was Greek, with [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was responsible.
There was one woman in our camp, the barrack [INAUDIBLE].
She would wake us up in the morning and share the food out.
She had to stay in the camp, in the barrack.
Oh yes.
That was-- it was the news of the day.
It was the best thing of the day.
Everybody got one thing full, that was it.
And as I said, if there was left, you got one number, you got a second portion.
Same.
They still got some.
Well, that got worse and worse, of course.
As I said, the lice, millions.
Yeah, well, in the wash house, yeah.
Yeah, we did every morning.
You know, we tried to be very hygienic ourselves.
We had a full body wash every morning.
Cold, cold water, soap that wouldn't foam.
But we did.
Not that I can think of.
There were, yeah.
There were people who kept the Friday night, on top of the bed, saved a piece of bread from during the week.
And put a sheet on like a white tablecloth and make a Shabbat.
Some people, because there were rabbis, very religious people.
But the rabbis had to pick and shuffle their work.
No, no, because I was there only from 7 January until April-- January '44 until say, March.
And in the meantime, I had the typhus.
There wasn't really much time.
As I said, [INAUDIBLE] made us brass bags.
And some people make brass of them, and some people threw them in the fire, in the potbelly, you know, just the heater.
And just when they came in, when the Germans came in, we were all working.
And I got into trouble with the other people there because I did too much.
Because I'm somebody, what I do, I'm always very fast with everything.
And they thought I did too much.
I was too fast.
Well, they went to [INAUDIBLE], you know?
Do as little as possible, of course.
And I did too much.
And they pour in some, and they made brass with some.
No, I was scared.
But would they have caught us, I would have gone just same, because I was in the group, whether I did it or no.
No, I don't think we would.
Yes.
Well, she didn't need any help.
I don't know how she did it, but she did.
She did.
Because her husband was also a cook.
She was just working in the kitchen, and her husband worked as a-- maybe he got permission.
I don't know.
But even though, you know, they say when you're in a coma, talk to the people.
They can hear it.
I believe that, because vaguely, I knew she was there.
And I was thinking myself, as long as I eat, I have to eat to survive.
I was thinking that while I was semi-- and apparently it did help.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Maybe not.
Now I've always heard, you know, when you're sick in the camps, as long as you can eat.
It must been my subconscious.
We didn't hear anything.
We didn't hear anything.
Well, later on when we were in the train, we heard it all.
I'll talk about the train later.
About March, I think it was.
Well, we knew we had to go.
Where to, we don't know.
We had to pack up.
And people starting to leaving.
And there was one kapo, and I said, please look after my girlfriend.
She's in that bed over there.
But I had my own people to look after.
And I didn't go.
I stayed until one of the doctor's wives said, come on, come on quick.
Because they were scared that the Russians were coming.
And they were scared for the Russians still.
So I had to leave the patients and went.
And I was lucky enough to get in a truck again, to the station.
And the cattle train was standing already.
And all prisoners-- non-Jewish prisoners in the uniforms, striped jackets-- were unloaded out of the train.
And we were put in, not clean, there were lice in already in the wagons.
They were, outside, there were heaps of beetroot, raw beetroot, and kohlrabi, which is like sweet-- you know, when you put in soup, those white things.
Sprouts?
No, no.
But they're raw, like carrots.
No, something like it.
Turnips, like turnips, yeah.
Heaps of them, and you could take that to take in the train.
We could-- later on we got a piece of bread.
That was it.
And so we went with about, I don't know, about 75 I think, in one wagon, sitting on the floor.
And I was still, coming out of the hospital, still to look after them.
And we went riding.
And every day, somebody died.
People died, left, right, and center of me.
The train would stop, and we would kind of throw them out, more or less.
They were buried on the spot or left there.
We had to go to the toilet, we were sitting at the side of the road, and the Germans just looking on with the gun on the shoulder.
And we could hear-- I had my 25th birthday on  the train.
Don't ask me, did I have a calendar, did I know the date?
I can't remember, but I knew it was my birthday.
I don't know why.
And sometimes the train had to stop because the airplanes were going over.
At one stop we put something, a sheet or something, on the train on top, with a Star of David on it, you know, so they would see it.
And we could hear shooting, that side and that side.
You know, the Americans and the Russians.
We did not know that our train was destined to have an accident in the River Elbe.
That was the plan.
But seeing that the Americans and the Russians came close, the guards got a bit scared and were a bit friendly towards us.
So the accident did not happen.
And one morning, knock knock at the door.
Open up, open up, we're free.
The Russians are here.
So we all went to the village.
I was not so lucky to get-- we had to go into the homes of the Germans, and me and two other--
No, we did not know we were in the River Elbe.
To another camp, that was all we knew.
Well, we didn't know this much, but there was one wagon with the leaders in it.
Like I gave you a piece of paper that would say that it was in the train, that it was signed by a couple of people.

Well, they were in the passenger's wagon, keeping-- because I had to go every day when somebody died, I had to go to that compartment and say, such and such died, such and such died, so that they could keep the lists, which was a good thing, of course.
Yes, the matron of the-- who was matron in the hospital.
She was there.
And, yeah.
We didn't notice anything with the guards.
Maybe the ones in the-- you know, the leaders might have had contact with them.
We were free.
We were free.
In the train?
Yeah.
You know, there was one woman with two grown up children-- well, grown up, maybe 12, 13 years-- and her husband died.
She was whole night, she was sitting there next to her dead husband.
Yeah.
It was terrible.
Yeah.
I had my locket in there, and I left it there.
Forgot it all.
Never saw it again.
And we had to go to the houses of the Germans.
Well, I wasn't that active, and the people I was with, you know, we took it easy.
And by the time we came in the village, most of the houses were taken.
And we found a house and just put a mattress on the floor.
And some people got nice bedrooms.
The cellars of those houses were full of food, bottled-- you know, bottled food.
They had plenty.
So we lived in that house until I got typhoid again, a different kind of typhoid.
Everybody got typhoid again.
Lots of people died, couldn't-- the food, they couldn't stand the food.
Stomach wasn't used to it.
They died.
Were you with anybody who died?
No.
And the Russians opened up a hospital.
But somehow, I didn't go to that hospital.
Then I got a little bit better.
And I could walk around.
And then one morning, the Russians are coming to shave our heads, you know, because of the lice.
So the Russians came in the front door of our house, and I went out the back.
And I walked and walked and walked all day.
I didn't want my head shaven.
That's something, you know, I don't want a shaven head.
And then there was somebody who, for a bit of food, he would clean your head.
A special comb.
I think it was my comb, but I lost it.
It was a special comb.
And I went there and she cleaned my head.
And right away, when we came in that town, and we went walking, walking, and there was somebody coming on a bike.
And it was the first Russian coming in, in the village.
And he was Jewish.
The first, on the bike.
And he couldn't understand that we didn't speak Yiddish.
You know Yiddish?
You've heard of that?
But in Holland we didn't.
And he couldn't understand being Jewish and not speaking Yiddish.
And we was the forerunner.
The forerunner to the whole Russian troops?
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Different group.
That was in the village.
The Russians that was on the way, in the train.
But that was the village, Trobitz.
And now they call it the Lost Transport.
Yeah, the train, the Lost Train, the Lost Transport.
I don't know.
Because we went without any purpose, driving around and around for two weeks.
Oh no, they had to go out.
They fled.
And we got rations off the Russians.
And they didn't-- didn't do anything to the Germans.
No special treatment, nothing.
Yeah.
T-R-O-E-B-I-T-Z, Troebitz.
Yes.
And then I got better again.
The doctor came a few times, yes.
And I got better.
And when they opened the hospital, and we had a little boy living with us.
And he got sick.
And I carried him to the truck to go to the hospital.
And also after I got better, we had to peel potatoes.
OK.
That's the end of Tape Three of the interview with Charlotte Melkman.
It's the 24th of March, 1995.
We are in Sydney, Australia.
